verses.text: Hezekiah does right like David, removes the high places, breaks the images, and even smashes the bronze serpent of Moses, now worshiped as 'Nehushtan.' He destroys a holy relic turned idol. When a good thing becomes an object of worship, true reform must break it.

Hezekiah does right like David, removes the high places, breaks the images, and even smashes the bronze serpent of Moses, now worshiped as 'Nehushtan.' He destroys a holy relic turned idol. When a good thing becomes an object of worship, true reform must break it.

Hezekiah trusts the LORD as none before or after among Judah's kings, clings to Him, and prospers, rebelling against Assyria. His strength is his trust. The secret of his greatness is not policy but a heart that held fast to God.

The narrative recalls how Samaria fell because Israel would not obey the LORD's covenant. The northern ruin is set beside Hezekiah's faith as a warning and a contrast. One kingdom perished in unbelief; the other will be saved through trust.

Sennacherib seizes Judah's fortified cities, and Hezekiah, faltering, pays a huge tribute, stripping the temple gold. Here even the good king buys peace by fear instead of faith. Yet his stumble is not his end; God will lead him back to trust.

Sennacherib sends a great army and the Rabshakeh to Jerusalem, who stands by the conduit and summons the king's officials. The threat now stands at the wall. The crisis of faith comes to a head outside the gates of the holy city.

Sennacherib sends a great army and the Rabshakeh to Jerusalem, who stands by the conduit and summons the king's officials. The threat now stands at the wall. The crisis of faith comes to a head outside the gates of the holy city.

The Rabshakeh sneers: in whom do you trust? Not Egypt, a broken reed; and if you say the LORD, did not Hezekiah remove His high places? He twists Hezekiah's very reforms into proof of weakness. The enemy loves to turn our obedience into an accusation.

He mocks Judah's tiny army and even claims the LORD sent him to destroy the land. Blasphemy dresses itself as piety. The proud will misuse God's own name to shake the faith of His people.
He mocks Judah's tiny army and even claims the LORD sent him to destroy the land. Blasphemy dresses itself as piety. The proud will misuse God's own name to shake the faith of His people.
He mocks Judah's tiny army and even claims the LORD sent him to destroy the land. Blasphemy dresses itself as piety. The proud will misuse God's own name to shake the faith of His people.

The officials beg him to speak Aramaic, not Hebrew, lest the people hear; but he answers all the more crudely, aiming his words at the men on the wall. He wants to spread fear and despair. The enemy targets the ordinary believer's courage, not just the leaders'.

The officials beg him to speak Aramaic, not Hebrew, lest the people hear; but he answers all the more crudely, aiming his words at the men on the wall. He wants to spread fear and despair. The enemy targets the ordinary believer's courage, not just the leaders'.

Rabshakeh shouts to the people: do not let Hezekiah deceive you; make peace with me and live well. He offers comfort in surrender. The tempter's sweetest lie is that giving up trust in God will make us safe and prosperous.

He blasphemes outright: has any nation's god delivered it from Assyria? Why should the LORD? He lumps the living God with the idols he has crushed. Pride that has toppled false gods imagines the true God is no different — a fatal mistake.

The people hold their peace, answering nothing, as Hezekiah commanded, and his officials come to him with torn clothes. They will not debate blasphemy; they take it to the king. Sometimes the wisest answer to mockery is silence and prayer, not argument.
